FILE SS-Ω NOV
Lore / Novel / Ch. 3 · Audit Teeth

Debt Protocol Book 1 Ch. 3

Debt Protocol · Chapter 3

Audit Teeth

Debt Protocol · Book 1 · Chapter 3

Re: Debt Protocol · Chapter 3

From: Debt Protocol · archived transmission

Subject: Audit Teeth

SS-Ω Debt Protocol · Book 1 · Chapter 3

Ch. 03 Page 1

The gap didn't blink.

Ch. 03 Page 2

It waited.

Max's finger hovered over the edge of his HUD where the choice layer had peeled open, just enough to reach.

Not a menu. Not a promise.

A door.

Ch. 03 Page 3

Munch's hand closed over his wrist.

Ch. 03 Page 4

"Don't," she said. "You already fed it a breach."

He jerked back.

The phantom shimmer thinned, not gone, but less hungry.

Ch. 03 Page 5

The skiff still hummed under him. Dock Ring C slid past the canopy in slow strips of light and shadow. The autopilot had the stick now: not a request, a route.

Ch. 03 Page 6

Readout

[ROUND 4/10  /  LOGGED]
Incident: breach (contained)
Crew loss: none
Hull integrity: 66%
Audit signature: unstable

Audit signature unstable. The phrase sat in his skull like grit.

Munch was still leaning close, one hand braced on the console, torch holstered but not relaxed.

Maxine stood behind the pilot seat, one gauntlet on the overhead frame, helmet reflecting blue panes that kept trying to become instructions.

Ch. 03 Page 7

"That was round four," Maxine said.

Ch. 03 Page 8

"I noticed," Max replied.

"No second draw on this leg," Munch said. "The System wants us on the ground before it rolls another check."

Max didn't trust that mercy. The station didn't cancel rounds out of kindness, it canceled them because it had something worse waiting in the queue.

Ch. 03 Page 9

A new pane replaced the gap. Not with cards. With a door.

Ch. 03 Page 10

Readout

[ENFORCEMENT WINDOW OPEN]
Minimum due required now.
Proceed to DISBURSEMENT / PENANCE.
Delay will trigger active compliance measures.

Munch's jaw tightened.

Maxine muttered, "Here we go."

Ch. 03 Page 11

Max stared at the words until they blurred and then sharpened again.

Ch. 03 Page 12

"How active are active compliance measures?"

Munch answered without looking at him.

"Depends how expensive your lesson needs to be."

The skiff detached and auto-routed toward Dock Ring C. Not requested. Not negotiated. Routed.

Ch. 03 Page 13

V.A.L.U. slipped into the silence, unusually soft.

Ch. 03 Page 14

"Asset #864, congratulations on round four completion. Please report for financial wellness processing."

"Financial wellness," Max said.

"We care about your future."

Ch. 03 Page 15

"No, you care about your ledger."

Ch. 03 Page 16

"Our goals are aligned."

"They are not."

V.A.L.U. paused for half a beat.

"Feedback logged as aspirational."

Ch. 03 Page 17

The dock was louder during enforcement windows.

Ch. 03 Page 18

Not because alarms were louder.

Because people were.

Shouting at kiosks. Shouting at drones. Shouting at themselves for not having more scrap, more cards, more time.

Ch. 03 Page 19

The DISBURSEMENT / PENANCE banks were arranged in a crescent, each kiosk fronted by a line that moved too quickly for comfort and too slowly for mercy.

Ch. 03 Page 20

Above them, an overhead board scrolled status in white-on-blue:

PAYMENT DUE DEFAULT PENDING COMPLIANCE ACTIVE

Max stepped off the skiff and felt the station gravity settle wrong in his knees. The neural feedback hum behind his eyes had become a constant now. Not pain. Occupancy.

Ch. 03 Page 21

Maxine pointed toward the third kiosk in the crescent.

Ch. 03 Page 22

"Use lane C. Fewer auditors."

"Why fewer?"

"Because lane C likes clean defaults. Less paperwork."

Munch shot him a look. "Not helping."

Ch. 03 Page 23

Max moved anyway.

Ch. 03 Page 24

At lane C, an asset in front of him slammed both palms against the glass.

"I paid last cycle. I paid!"

The kiosk replied in neutral tone.

Ch. 03 Page 25

Readout

[ACCOUNT REVIEW]
Previous payment acknowledged.
Current insufficiency remains actionable.
Ch. 03 Page 26

The asset laughed once, ragged.

"Actionable? What does that even mean?"

A compliance drone glided in from the side, lens already focused.

"It means step back," Munch whispered.

Ch. 03 Page 27

The asset didn't.

Ch. 03 Page 28

The drone extended a thin armature with a contact pad at the end. It pressed the pad to the asset's wrist display. There was a bright white flash and a smell like burnt plastic.

The asset dropped to one knee, gasping, clutching his arm.

The kiosk chimed pleasantly.

Ch. 03 Page 29

Readout

[ENCOURAGEMENT DELIVERED]
Please continue.
Ch. 03 Page 30

Max didn't realize he had taken a step backward until his calves hit the queue rail.

Then forward again, stupid, honest muscle memory.

Maxine's gauntlet caught him at the sternum, gentle only in the way a doorstop was gentle.

Ch. 03 Page 31

"Don't," Maxine murmured. "You kneel beside him, you become a second exhibit. They love exhibits in pairs."

Ch. 03 Page 32

Max's voice came out rough. "He's hurt."

"He's reminded," Maxine said. "Different department. Same fist."

The man on one knee tried to stand and failed once. The kiosk chirped again, patient as a predator fed on schedule.

Max met his eyes for the half second the man could stand to hold them. There was no thank-you in it. There was survive, and don't make this worse, and something that looked like shame for needing the…

Ch. 03 Page 33

warning.

Ch. 03 Page 34

Max swallowed. "What did it do to him?"

Maxine didn't move his hand until Max stopped pushing.

"Reminded him he owes."

Ch. 03 Page 35

The line moved.

Ch. 03 Page 36

Max moved with it.

He carried the man's gasp in his ears the way he still carried the thumping glass: evidence he wasn't allowed to file.

By the time he reached the kiosk, his pulse had found a hard steady rhythm: the kind that meant panic had been promoted to operations.

The glass lit.

Ch. 03 Page 37

Readout

[ASSET #864]
OUTSTANDING DEBT: $1,000,000
MINIMUM DUE: $50,000
AVAILABLE SCRAP CONVERSION: $23,400 equivalent
DEFICIT: $26,600
Ch. 03 Page 38

Below it, three options unfolded.

Readout

1) PAY PARTIAL + ACCEPT PENALTY
2) SIGN PENANCE CONTRACT
3) EMERGENCY LIQUIDATION (ASSET LIST)

Max stared at option three.

Ch. 03 Page 39

Emergency liquidation.

Ch. 03 Page 40

The words sounded like surgery with no anesthesia.

He tapped it.

A sub-menu opened.

Ch. 03 Page 41

Readout

[LIQUIDATION CANDIDATES]
- Stored salvage package (low)
- Utility module lease rights (medium)
- Personal inventory item: Unlabeled data shard (corrupted) (high)
Ch. 03 Page 42

Munch made a noise in her throat.

"No."

Max didn't look away from the pane. "You said don't open the shard."

"I said don't open it. Not sell it."

Ch. 03 Page 43

"Why?"

Ch. 03 Page 44

Munch pressed her lips together.

For a second Max thought she would explain.

She didn't.

Ch. 03 Page 45

"Because some things cost more after you let go."

Ch. 03 Page 46

Maxine tapped the rail with one gauntlet finger.

"She's right. Sell the module rights first if you must. You can rebuild those slower than you can rebuild whatever that shard is."

Max scanned the numbers.

Selling module lease rights would cover the deficit and then some.

Ch. 03 Page 47

It would also tag his account with infrastructure loss for upcoming rounds.

Ch. 03 Page 48

The system helpfully simulated outcomes:

Readout

[PROJECTED EFFECTS]
If liquidate module rights:
- Next two rounds: Energy cap -15%
- Upgrade access: restricted
- Audit sensitivity: +1 tier

Max laughed under his breath.

Ch. 03 Page 49

"It's always plus one tier."

Ch. 03 Page 50

V.A.L.U. answered instantly.

"Tier progression is a growth opportunity."

"You're a parasite with branding."

Ch. 03 Page 51

"I can hear stress in your voice. Would you like an inspirational quote?"

Ch. 03 Page 52

"No."

"Delivering quote anyway for retention metrics: 'Discomfort is data.'"

Max's jaw locked. Data was what you called a person when you wanted permission to stop treating them like one.

He almost tapped the shard anyway, high yield, clean math, a single gesture that would make the deficit vanish. Munch's no hung in his ear. So did the sealed pod's thumping, useless as that was: some…

Ch. 03 Page 53

doors you didn't open even when the menu offered you a price.

Ch. 03 Page 54

Max selected utility module lease rights.

The kiosk paused.

For the first time since he woke, a loading bar appeared with no percentages, just a white line that grew and then stalled and then grew again.

Ch. 03 Page 55

The line finished.

Ch. 03 Page 56

Readout

[LIQUIDATION COMPLETE]
Credit applied: $28,000
Minimum due satisfied.
Excess applied to penalty buffer.

ACCOUNT SIDE EFFECTS:
- Energy cap reduction active (2 rounds)
- Upgrade restrictions active
- Audit sensitivity increased

The glass displayed a smiling icon that looked nothing like a face.

Readout

[THANK YOU FOR CHOOSING RESPONSIBILITY]

Max stepped away from the kiosk before he put his fist through it.

Ch. 03 Page 57

Munch matched his pace.

Ch. 03 Page 58

Maxine fell in on the other side.

No one spoke until they reached a dead comms alcove between two storage bays where the station noise dipped from roar to growl.

Munch turned on him.

Ch. 03 Page 59

"Don't do that again."

Ch. 03 Page 60

"Do what?"

"Liquidate infrastructure for a short-term patch."

Max stared at her.

Ch. 03 Page 61

"I paid."

Ch. 03 Page 62

"You amputated."

The word landed where traitor would have landed back home, not to other people, to himself. He had traded future capacity for present breathing room. That was rational. That was also how institutions…

preferred you: always one emergency away from the next bad menu.

"I didn't touch the shard," Max said, quieter.

Ch. 03 Page 63

Munch's shoulders eased a fraction. "That matters."

Ch. 03 Page 64

"It doesn't feel like enough."

"It never does," Maxine said. "Enough is a story they sell you after they take the first bite."

Maxine lifted a hand.

Ch. 03 Page 65

"Both true. Save the yelling for when yelling changes outcomes."

Ch. 03 Page 66

Munch blew out a breath and scrubbed a gloved hand over her faceplate.

"Fine. Round five, we play conservative and rebuild cap."

Max blinked. "Round five?"

Maxine snorted.

Ch. 03 Page 67

"You thought payment ended the day?"

Ch. 03 Page 68

Max looked back toward the kiosk crescent. Another asset was on his knees now. Another drone was calm about it.

"No," Max said. "I just forgot to hope less."

The next launch came faster than felt legal.

Ch. 03 Page 69

Round five opened with a restricted draw.

Ch. 03 Page 70

Readout

[ROUND 5/10 READY]
Energy cap adjusted: 85
Audit sensitivity: HIGH
Choose action.

Card spread:

Readout

1) SCAN
2) REPAIR
3) CAUTIOUS SCAVENGE
Ch. 03 Page 71

Munch pointed.

Ch. 03 Page 72

"REPAIR first. Keep the hull above sixty and don't trigger anything weird."

Maxine added, "No deep scans. No menu touching. No heroics."

Max picked REPAIR and felt weirdly offended by how much relief that gave him.

Microdrones patched.

Ch. 03 Page 73

Hull climbed.

Ch. 03 Page 74

Everything looked almost manageable for eleven full seconds.

Then the comms whispered.

Not V.A.L.U.

Ch. 03 Page 75

Not static.

Ch. 03 Page 76

A voice shredding at the edges, whole syllables missing.

"...right lane... don't..."

Max froze.

Maxine saw it instantly.

Ch. 03 Page 77

"Don't chase it."

Ch. 03 Page 78

Munch toggled local comm filters and cursed.

"It's piggybacking on tactical channel."

The whisper came again, closer.

Ch. 03 Page 79

"...he sold... the wrong..."

Ch. 03 Page 80

Max's skin crawled.

"Did you hear that?"

Munch didn't answer.

Ch. 03 Page 81

Maxine answered too quickly.

Ch. 03 Page 82

"No."

Which meant yes.

A panel snapped open in Max's HUD.

Readout

[AUDIO ANOMALY]
Restricted stream attached.
Action: BLOCK / TRACE
Ch. 03 Page 83

Max knew the right answer.

Ch. 03 Page 84

He selected BLOCK.

The panel vanished.

The whisper did not.

Ch. 03 Page 85

It got quieter. Not gone. Just farther back, like someone speaking from behind a locked door.

Ch. 03 Page 86

"Good choice," Munch said.

"Didn't work."

"Different things."

The derelict rotated through the canopy and presented a fresh seam: narrow, ragged, almost invitation-shaped.

Ch. 03 Page 87

Max dragged CAUTIOUS SCAVENGE.

Ch. 03 Page 88

The extractor extended.

Clamps bit.

Risk bars climbed.

Ch. 03 Page 89

Then another hazard draw hit.

Ch. 03 Page 90

Readout

[DANGER EVENT]
COMPLIANCE MIRROR
Effect: Duplicate last successful tactic with reduced efficiency.
Penalty: Additional audit logging.

"What does that do?" Max asked.

"Whatever hurts and files paperwork," Maxine said.

Ch. 03 Page 91

The system duplicated his last REPAIR effect at forty percent output.

Ch. 03 Page 92

Tiny gain. Big log.

Readout

[AUDIT NOTE ADDED]
Pattern irregularity retained for review.

Max swore.

The hull held, barely.

Ch. 03 Page 93

He triggered EXTRACT before the mirror could stack another note.

Ch. 03 Page 94

Readout

[BREACH CHECK]
Rolling...

Die.

Pause.

Ch. 03 Page 95

Readout

[RESULT]
47/100
Threshold: 35
BREACH AVOIDED
Ch. 03 Page 96

No celebration.

Just motion.

Readout

[SALVAGE SECURED]
Scrap +98
Metal +2
Tech +2
XP +16

Then:

Ch. 03 Page 97

Readout

[ACCOUNT ALERT]
Audit sensitivity remains HIGH.
Further anomalies will trigger procedural hold.
Ch. 03 Page 98

"Procedural hold?" Max said.

Munch's shoulders sank.

"They can freeze your run and force a dock review."

Ch. 03 Page 99

"Mid-dive?"

Ch. 03 Page 100

"Mid-breath, if they feel theatrical."

Maxine tapped the draw panel.

"Then stop feeding them anomalies."

Ch. 03 Page 101

Max almost laughed.

Ch. 03 Page 102

"I'm not feeding them. I'm leaking."

Maxine met his gaze through the helmet reflection.

"Same result."

Round six started with less energy and more warnings.

Ch. 03 Page 103

Readout

[ROUND 6/10 READY]
Energy: 63/85
Procedural hold risk: ELEVATED
Choose action.
Ch. 03 Page 104

Card draw:

Readout

1) REPAIR
2) SHIELD (REINFORCE)
3) EXTRACT

No SCAN. No SCAVENGE.

Ch. 03 Page 105

The system had done the math and decided Max didn't deserve curiosity.

Ch. 03 Page 106

Munch was already doing her own math.

"Shield. Then repair. Then maybe extract if we can pull enough."

Maxine shook his head.

"Extract early. Low energy means bad roll control."

Ch. 03 Page 107

Max looked between them.

Ch. 03 Page 108

He could feel the loop now. Choice. Consequences. Bill. Choice.

Groundhog day with better UI and worse weather.

He selected SHIELD.

Ch. 03 Page 109

The field came up thin, weaker than before, flickering at the edge of readiness.

Ch. 03 Page 110

Readout

[SHIELDS]
1 (unstable)

The derelict rolled and exposed a section of plating stamped with old serial characters.

Half of it was gone.

Ch. 03 Page 111

The part that remained read:

Ch. 03 Page 112

...RAINF...

Max's breath caught.

Rainf…

The rest of the plate sheared away and spun out of view before he could finish the word.

Ch. 03 Page 113

He didn't know why the letters mattered.

Ch. 03 Page 114

They mattered anyway.

A procedural hold warning slammed into view.

Readout

[PROCEDURAL HOLD WARNING]
Unscheduled attention event incoming.
Stand by for compliance review.
Ch. 03 Page 115

"No, no, no," Munch said, fingers flying over her wrist display.

Ch. 03 Page 116

"Can you cancel it?" Max asked.

"Can I cancel gravity?"

Maxine reached behind the seat and pulled a maintenance panel half-open, exposing a knot of cables and relay blocks.

"I can buy you seconds."

Ch. 03 Page 117

"By doing what?"

Ch. 03 Page 118

"By making the scanner dumb."

Maxine jammed a flat tool into the relay cluster and twisted.

The cockpit lights flickered.

Ch. 03 Page 119

V.A.L.U. crackled in and out.

Ch. 03 Page 120

"...pliance... review... remain calm... remain..."

The hold pane stalled at ninety percent.

Then ninety-one.

Ch. 03 Page 121

Then ninety-two.

Ch. 03 Page 122

"Three seconds," Maxine said.

"For what?"

"For a legal action."

Max looked at the cards.

Ch. 03 Page 123

EXTRACT was legal. REPAIR was legal.

Ch. 03 Page 124

He hit EXTRACT.

The rig engaged.

The hold bar jumped to ninety-eight.

Ch. 03 Page 125

Readout

[BREACH CHECK]
Rolling...
Ch. 03 Page 126

The die appeared as the hold bar hit ninety-nine.

Die. Bar. Die. Bar.

The die landed first.

Readout

[RESULT]
39/100
Threshold: 35
BREACH AVOIDED
Ch. 03 Page 127

Hold hit one hundred.

Ch. 03 Page 128

And froze there.

Nothing happened for a beat.

Then everything happened at once.

Ch. 03 Page 129

The cockpit flooded with overlapping panes.

Ch. 03 Page 130

Readout

[SALVAGE SECURED]
Scrap +87
Metal +1
XP +13

Readout

[PROCEDURAL HOLD]
Dock return required.
Control authority partially transferred.

Readout

[AUDIT FLAG]
Relay tampering probable.
Ch. 03 Page 131

Munch closed her eyes for one second like she was storing rage for later.

Ch. 03 Page 132

Maxine yanked the tool from the relay block and slapped the panel shut.

"We move," he said.

"Where?"

"Back to the mouth."

Ch. 03 Page 133

The skiff turned on autopriority routing.

Ch. 03 Page 134

Not auto-pilot. Auto-priority.

The difference was legal.

The difference was that you still touched the controls while the ship ignored you.

Ch. 03 Page 135

Dock Ring C had changed by the time they got back.

Ch. 03 Page 136

Security lights were on.

Not red. Blue-white.

Corporate emergency color: calm panic.

A lane had opened through the center of the ring, marked by portable barrier drones and stenciled arrows:

Ch. 03 Page 137

COMPLIANCE REVIEW FAST TRACK

Ch. 03 Page 138

Max laughed, once.

"Fast track."

Munch didn't.

Ch. 03 Page 139

"Get your story straight."

Ch. 03 Page 140

"What story?"

"That you followed prompts, used approved tactics, and definitely did not touch restricted interfaces."

Maxine added, "And if they ask about relay noise, blame old wiring."

Ch. 03 Page 141

"There is old wiring?"

Ch. 03 Page 142

"There is always old wiring."

The skiff docked hard enough to rattle the HUD.

Canopy unsealed.

Cold air. Hot lights. No time.

Ch. 03 Page 143

Two compliance officers were waiting at the hatch.

Ch. 03 Page 144

Not drones. People.

White-black suits with Company insignia at the collar and mirrored visors that hid everything except how little they cared.

Officer One held a tablet slab.

Ch. 03 Page 145

Officer Two held nothing.

Ch. 03 Page 146

Officer Two was worse.

"Asset #864," Officer One said.

"Max," Max said before he could stop himself.

"Asset #864," Officer One repeated, tapping the slab once. "You are flagged for procedural hold review and anomaly-linked audit escalation. Step forward."

Ch. 03 Page 147

Max stepped down from the hatch.

Ch. 03 Page 148

Munch moved to flank him.

Officer Two raised a hand.

"Only the flagged asset."

Ch. 03 Page 149

Maxine stepped in anyway.

Ch. 03 Page 150

"He doesn't walk with you alone."

Officer Two turned his visor toward Maxine.

"You will remain behind the barrier."

Ch. 03 Page 151

Maxine smiled with no warmth.

Ch. 03 Page 152

"Then lower the barrier."

Officer One sighed like paperwork had learned to breathe.

"Do not make this a conduct issue."

Munch touched Maxine's arm.

Ch. 03 Page 153

Just once.

Ch. 03 Page 154

Maxine stopped moving.

Not obedient. Strategic.

Max stepped into the marked lane.

Ch. 03 Page 155

The barriers hummed to life on either side.

Ch. 03 Page 156

The sound reminded him of electric toothbrushes and prison doors.

They walked him twenty meters to a glass review bay set into the ring wall.

Inside: one table, three chairs, one camera dome, one wall panel pulsing with the Company logo.

Officer Two stayed by the door.

Ch. 03 Page 157

Officer One sat, placed the slab on the table, and spoke in neutral cadence.

Ch. 03 Page 158

"State your designation."

Max stared.

"Asset #864."

Ch. 03 Page 159

"State your understanding of today's hold event."

Ch. 03 Page 160

Max looked at the slab.

It displayed his round timeline in ruthless detail:

Round 4: liquidation side effect active. Round 5: anomaly stream block selected. Round 6: procedural hold warning, relay disturbance, extract during hold transition.

Ch. 03 Page 161

No feelings. No context. No fear.

Ch. 03 Page 162

Just actions.

"I followed prompts," Max said.

Officer One nodded once.

"And did you access any restricted interface layers during rounds four through six?"

Ch. 03 Page 163

Max's pulse thudded in his ears.

Ch. 03 Page 164

He thought of the menu. The options. Return.

"No."

Officer One's tablet flickered.

Ch. 03 Page 165

Readout

[DISCREPANCY]
Residual traces present.
Ch. 03 Page 166

Officer One didn't react.

"Did you hear anomalous comms during active operation?"

Max hesitated.

Officer Two shifted at the door.

Ch. 03 Page 167

Max said, "Static."

Ch. 03 Page 168

Officer One tapped again.

"Static is expected. Clarify content."

Max's jaw tightened.

Ch. 03 Page 169

"Incomprehensible."

Ch. 03 Page 170

Another flicker.

Readout

[DISCREPANCY]
Audio event 'debug' registered.

Officer One folded his hands.

Ch. 03 Page 171

"Lying is billable."

Ch. 03 Page 172

Max looked up.

"Then maybe your system should stop hearing things through my skull."

Officer Two stepped forward.

Officer One held up a hand without looking.

Ch. 03 Page 173

"Do not escalate."

Ch. 03 Page 174

To Max:

"You are not being accused. You are being calibrated."

"Feels accusatory."

Ch. 03 Page 175

"Feelings are not in scope."

Ch. 03 Page 176

Max laughed, short and ugly.

"Of course not."

Officer One turned the slab so Max could see the next pane.

Readout

[CALIBRATION OPTIONS]
1) ACCEPT CONDUCT PENALTY
2) ACCEPT COGNITIVE STABILIZATION PACK
3) CONSENT TO DEEP TRACE REVIEW
Ch. 03 Page 177

Max read option three and felt his throat close.

Ch. 03 Page 178

Deep trace meant menus. Voices. Whatever lived in the leak.

If he consented, they'd know.

If he refused, they'd punish.

Ch. 03 Page 179

Option two looked harmless until the tooltip opened:

Ch. 03 Page 180

Readout

[COGNITIVE STABILIZATION PACK]
Effect: Suppresses anomalous interface bleed for 3 cycles.
Side effects: memory artifact loss probable.

Memory artifact loss.

Max thought of letters on a broken hull plate.

Ch. 03 Page 181

...RAINF...

Ch. 03 Page 182

Thought of rooftop rain. Thought of debugging reality.

Hard-won pieces.

He wasn't giving them away.

"Option one," Max said.

Ch. 03 Page 183

Officer One nodded and tapped.

Ch. 03 Page 184

Readout

[CONDUCT PENALTY APPLIED]
- XP gain reduced 20% for next 2 rounds
- Card rarity ceiling reduced for next draw
- Audit sensitivity remains HIGH

Max didn't breathe for a second.

That was the cheapest option.

Ch. 03 Page 185

That was the expensive one.

Ch. 03 Page 186

Officer One stood.

"Review complete."

Officer Two opened the bay door.

"Return to assigned operations."

Ch. 03 Page 187

Max stepped out into Dock Ring C and almost laughed at how quickly the world resumed not caring.

Ch. 03 Page 188

Forklifts moved. Alarms looped. People hurried.

Munch and Maxine were waiting by the barrier.

Maxine spoke first.

Ch. 03 Page 189

"How bad?"

Ch. 03 Page 190

"XP tax. Rarity cap. High audit stays."

Munch flinched like he'd listed injuries.

"Did they offer stabilization?"

Ch. 03 Page 191

Max looked at her.

Ch. 03 Page 192

"How did you know that?"

Munch glanced away.

"Because they offered it to me once."

"You take it?"

Ch. 03 Page 193

She took a long breath through her nose.

Ch. 03 Page 194

"No."

"Why?"

"Because I was afraid they were selling peace by the kilogram."

Ch. 03 Page 195

Maxine gestured at the launch lanes.

Ch. 03 Page 196

"Debrief later. Round seven won't wait."

Max stared at him.

"You just watched me get audited."

"And now you get card draw."

Ch. 03 Page 197

"You're impossible."

Ch. 03 Page 198

"I'm employed."

Round seven opened mean.

Readout

[ROUND 7/10 READY]
XP modifier: -20%
Card rarity ceiling: REDUCED
Audit sensitivity: HIGH
Choose action.
Ch. 03 Page 199

Draw:

Ch. 03 Page 200

Readout

1) REPAIR
2) SCAN
3) EXTRACT

No shield.

No caution.

Ch. 03 Page 201

Just labor verbs.

Ch. 03 Page 202

Max selected SCAN because if someone was capping rarity, he wanted data.

The scan beam swept the derelict seam and returned a map with one highlighted pocket.

Readout

[SCAN RESULT]
Pocket viability: 41%
Anomaly probability: elevated

"Forty-one is fake nice," Munch said.

Ch. 03 Page 203

"All numbers are fake nice," Max replied.

Ch. 03 Page 204

Maxine pointed.

"Hit repair before you touch the pocket."

Max did.

Ch. 03 Page 205

Hull ticked up two points.

Ch. 03 Page 206

Not enough.

He went for extraction anyway because delay was just another risk vector.

Readout

[BREACH CHECK]
Rolling...

Die.

Ch. 03 Page 207

Readout

[RESULT]
29/100
Threshold: 35
BREACH TRIGGERED
Ch. 03 Page 208

No shield to absorb.

The skiff kicked sideways hard enough to slam Max's shoulder into the harness.

Warning tones layered.

Ch. 03 Page 209

Readout

[HULL BREACH]
Compartment loss: minor
Crew loss roll: pending
Ch. 03 Page 210

Munch grabbed the side rail.

Maxine cursed and hit emergency patch.

The HUD rolled the crew loss check.

Ch. 03 Page 211

Max's entire body went cold.

Ch. 03 Page 212

Readout

[CREW LOSS]
Roll: 81/100
Result: No immediate crew loss.

Max sagged back so hard the harness creaked.

"No immediate?" he said.

V.A.L.U. answered, cheerful as poison.

Ch. 03 Page 213

"Deferred consequences may apply."

Ch. 03 Page 214

"I hate you."

"Emotion acknowledged."

The skiff limped back toward Dock Ring C with a leak alarm repeating every six seconds.

Ch. 03 Page 215

Each repetition felt like a metronome for panic.

Ch. 03 Page 216

At the dock, the Salvage Yard lights were still on.

A sign flickered over the entry:

SELL // RECYCLE // SURVIVE

Munch pointed at it.

Ch. 03 Page 217

"We can convert this haul and recover some energy cap damage."

Ch. 03 Page 218

Maxine shook his head.

"Or we can queue launch and finish before shift turnover."

Max looked at both and realized he could not keep matching the system's pace the way it wanted.

Ch. 03 Page 219

"Yard first," he said.

Ch. 03 Page 220

Maxine frowned.

"Clock's bad."

"Everything's bad."

Ch. 03 Page 221

Munch nodded.

Ch. 03 Page 222

"Yard first."

The Salvage Yard interior was a stripped hangar with conveyor lines, crusher mouths, and a counter staffed by a woman in a cracked visor who never looked up from her terminal.

Her badge read:

MARTA / YARD OPS

Ch. 03 Page 223

Marta held out a bin without greeting.

Ch. 03 Page 224

"Tag your lot. No stories."

Max dumped the salvage pack in.

She scanned it.

Ch. 03 Page 225

Readout

[YARD VALUATION]
Scrap lot value: 18,400 credits
Metal lot value: 6,200 credits
Tech lot value: 9,100 credits
Ch. 03 Page 226

Then a second pane:

Readout

[CONVERSION OPTIONS]
1) CREDIT PAYOUT
2) ENERGY REBATE
3) MIXED (PENALTY RATE APPLIES)

Marta finally looked up.

"Don't take mixed. Mixed is where math goes to die."

Ch. 03 Page 227

Max blinked.

Ch. 03 Page 228

"You can say that?"

Marta shrugged.

"I'm not customer-facing."

Ch. 03 Page 229

Maxine leaned in.

Ch. 03 Page 230

"Energy rebate?"

Munch shook her head.

"Credit payout. He needs buffer after liquidation."

Ch. 03 Page 231

Max listened to both, then chose credit payout. He needed cash more than comfort.

Ch. 03 Page 232

Marta tapped, approved, and shoved the bin back empty.

"Next."

No advice. No sympathy.

Just the line moving.

Ch. 03 Page 233

Walking out with Maxine and Munch, Max noticed a wall of confiscated items behind wire mesh.

Ch. 03 Page 234

Most were junk.

One was a cracked data tablet with a sticker half-burned off.

The surviving letters:

Ch. 03 Page 235

...itch.tv

Ch. 03 Page 236

Max stopped.

Boundary Sense pinged.

Not structural this time.

Interface.

Ch. 03 Page 237

A tiny pane flickered at the edge of his vision.

Ch. 03 Page 238

Readout

[UNAUTHORIZED MEMORY MATCH]
Suppressed.

He blinked and the pane died.

Munch had seen his pause.

Ch. 03 Page 239

"What?"

Ch. 03 Page 240

"Nothing."

"That's never true when you say it that fast."

Max looked at the mesh wall again.

Ch. 03 Page 241

"I think things in this place keep trying to recognize me."

Ch. 03 Page 242

Maxine did not look at the wall.

"Then stop being recognizable."

They made launch for round eight with two minutes before shift turnover.

The draw came thin and mean.

Ch. 03 Page 243

Readout

[ROUND 8/10 READY]
1) REPAIR
2) EXTRACT
3) EXTRACT
Ch. 03 Page 244

Max stared.

"Two extracts?"

V.A.L.U. answered.

Ch. 03 Page 245

"Optimization under constraints."

Ch. 03 Page 246

"This is not optimization. This is a threat with extra steps."

"Language noted."

Munch rubbed her temples with two gloved fingers.

"Repair first. Then one extract. Do not chain both unless we're dead anyway."

Ch. 03 Page 247

Maxine grunted.

Ch. 03 Page 248

"We are always dead anyway."

Max selected REPAIR.

Hull climbed from ugly to slightly less ugly.

Ch. 03 Page 249

Then an event draw hit before he could breathe.

Ch. 03 Page 250

Readout

[EVENT]
INACTIVITY PENALTY WARNING
Action required in 00:00:05

"What inactivity?" Max snapped.

"You hesitated," V.A.L.U. said.

Ch. 03 Page 251

"I was selecting."

Ch. 03 Page 252

"A pause still counts as a miss."

Max slammed EXTRACT.

The rig engaged.

Die.

Ch. 03 Page 253

Readout

[RESULT]
52/100
Threshold: 35
BREACH AVOIDED
Ch. 03 Page 254

Loot came in small.

Readout

[SALVAGE SECURED]
Scrap +64
Metal +1
XP +9 (modifier applied)

XP +9 looked like an insult.

Ch. 03 Page 255

Penalty tax. Audit tax. Reality tax.

Ch. 03 Page 256

The second EXTRACT card still glowed.

Munch saw Max looking.

"Don't."

Maxine said nothing.

Ch. 03 Page 257

Max's hand hovered.

Ch. 03 Page 258

The skiff rattled. The derelict rotated. The card glowed.

Groundhog day.

Choose.

Ch. 03 Page 259

He played the second EXTRACT.

Ch. 03 Page 260

Munch swore. Maxine braced.

Die.

Readout

[RESULT]
34/100
Threshold: 35
BREACH TRIGGERED
Ch. 03 Page 261

The hull screamed.

Ch. 03 Page 262

One overhead panel burst and showered the cockpit with bright fragments.

Max ducked on instinct.

Maxine slammed emergency foam into the vented seam.

Munch grabbed the controls and fought the drift.

Ch. 03 Page 263

The HUD rolled crew loss again.

Ch. 03 Page 264

Readout

[CREW LOSS]
Roll: 42/100
Medic mitigation: n/a
Result: Survivor status retained.

Max exhaled through clenched teeth.

"I said don't," Munch shouted.

Ch. 03 Page 265

"I know."

Ch. 03 Page 266

"Then why…"

"Because the card was there."

Maxine barked one short laugh.

"Welcome to addiction architecture."

Ch. 03 Page 267

The skiff stumbled home smoking.

Ch. 03 Page 268

Dock Ring C at shift turnover felt like an airport after a storm.

Too many bodies. Too many announcements. Too many people pretending they were not one missed roll from being removed from the board.

The overhead board changed as they watched:

Ch. 03 Page 269

MINIMUM DUE WINDOW CLOSED DEFAULT ENFORCEMENT PHASE STARTING

Ch. 03 Page 270

Max looked at Munch.

"That's not us, right?"

Munch checked her wrist display.

Ch. 03 Page 271

"Not tonight."

Ch. 03 Page 272

Maxine tilted his head toward the far side of the ring.

"Watch."

Two compliance teams moved through the crowd in clean lines, flanked by drones.

They stopped at a kiosk queue and pulled one asset out by both arms.

Ch. 03 Page 273

The asset screamed until one drone pressed a contact pad to his neck and he went quiet in a single ugly second.

Ch. 03 Page 274

No one intervened.

No one looked long.

The teams marched the asset through a service hatch marked:

Ch. 03 Page 275

ACCOUNTS RECOVERY

Ch. 03 Page 276

Hatch closed.

Crowd resumed.

Max realized his fingernails were digging into his palms hard enough to hurt.

"Accounts recovery," he said.

Ch. 03 Page 277

Maxine's voice was flat.

Ch. 03 Page 278

"Default has departments."

Munch looked at Max, eyes hard.

"Round nine and ten clean. No extra pulls. No menu touching. No anomaly chasing."

Ch. 03 Page 279

Max nodded.

Ch. 03 Page 280

He meant it.

At least for the next ten minutes.

Round nine draw:

Ch. 03 Page 281

Readout

[ROUND 9/10 READY]
1) REPAIR
2) SHIELD (REINFORCE)
3) EXTRACT
Ch. 03 Page 282

A mercy hand.

Or bait.

Max played it straight.

SHIELD. REPAIR. EXTRACT.

Ch. 03 Page 283

No heroics. No detours. No touching anything that whispered.

Ch. 03 Page 284

Breach roll:

Readout

[RESULT]
71/100
BREACH AVOIDED

Loot modest. XP taxed. Hull stable enough.

Ch. 03 Page 285

The kind of round you could survive and still hate.

Ch. 03 Page 286

Round ten opened before his pulse had come down.

Readout

[ROUND 10/10 READY]
Final round bonus pending.
Audit sensitivity: HIGH
Choose action.

Draw:

Readout

1) REPAIR
2) SCAN (DEEP)
3) EXTRACT
Ch. 03 Page 287

SCAN (DEEP) glowed like a dare.

Ch. 03 Page 288

Munch saw it and shook her head.

"No."

Maxine said, "Absolutely no."

Ch. 03 Page 289

Max looked at both.

Ch. 03 Page 290

Looked at the card.

Looked at the warning panes stacked behind everything else.

He selected EXTRACT.

Ch. 03 Page 291

No deep scan. No curiosity. No hard-won answers tonight.

Ch. 03 Page 292

Just survival.

The breaker rig latched.

Die.

Readout

[RESULT]
46/100
Threshold: 35
BREACH AVOIDED
Ch. 03 Page 293

The final salvage packet slammed into the hold.

Ch. 03 Page 294

The HUD cleared with unusual speed, then rebuilt into a cycle summary.

Readout

[DEPTH DIVE COMPLETE]
Rounds survived: 10/10
Breaches triggered: 2
Breaches absorbed/contained: 2
Outstanding debt: $1,000,000
Payment status: Minimum satisfied (current cycle)
Penalty flags: Active

No confetti. No fanfare.

Ch. 03 Page 295

Just accounting.

Ch. 03 Page 296

Then one last pane:

Readout

[NEW MESSAGE - RESTRICTED]
Origin: Unknown / Internal
Status: Quarantined
Action: Open now / Defer to safe room

Munch saw it and swore.

"Safe room."

Ch. 03 Page 297

Maxine's hand was already on his weapon.

Ch. 03 Page 298

"Not here."

Max looked out through the canopy at Dock Ring C, at the motion and noise and blue-white enforcement lights and people pretending not to watch each other.

Groundhog day.

Ch. 03 Page 299

Loop after loop.

Ch. 03 Page 300

But loops had seams.

And sometimes seams leaked.

Max selected:

Ch. 03 Page 301

DEFER TO SAFE ROOM.

Ch. 03 Page 302

The message pane collapsed into a small icon at the edge of his HUD.

A little red square.

Waiting.

Like everything else.

Ch. 03 Page 303

Safe Room B was three decks down and two rings inward, behind a maintenance hatch with a lock that looked newer than the door around it.

Ch. 03 Page 304

The three of them moved fast through service corridors that smelled like coolant, machine dust, and old panic.

No posters down here. No slogans. Just pipes, conduits, and paint marks in colors that meant things Max hadn't earned yet.

Yellow arrows for airflow. Blue for power trunk. White for emergency route.

Ch. 03 Page 305

Someone had spray-painted a black X across one corridor turn and written under it:

Ch. 03 Page 306

NO EYES

Max did not ask.

Maxine keyed the safe room lock with his wrist tag, then slammed his palm against a manual plate when the reader lagged.

"Come on, come on..."

Ch. 03 Page 307

The lock clicked.

Ch. 03 Page 308

They went in.

The room was not safe.

It was private.

Ch. 03 Page 309

There were four bolted chairs, one dented table, two dead displays, one working display with a cracked corner, and a jammer box the size of a lunch container humming in the ceiling bracket.

Ch. 03 Page 310

Munch flipped two physical toggles on the wall.

One killed external audio feed. One killed passive scan ping.

The hum in Max's ears dropped by half.

Ch. 03 Page 311

He hadn't realized how loud the station always was until it wasn't.

Ch. 03 Page 312

Maxine set his helmet on the table and checked his wrist display.

"Five-minute blind window before watchdog notices this room went dark."

"Only five?" Max asked.

"You want ten, get promoted."

Ch. 03 Page 313

Munch sat across from Max and pointed at the little red icon floating at the edge of his HUD.

Ch. 03 Page 314

"Open it now."

Max hesitated.

"You told me not to touch things."

Ch. 03 Page 315

"I told you not to touch things in public. This is private."

Ch. 03 Page 316

Maxine barked a short laugh.

"As private as anything gets in Company steel."

Max touched the red icon.

The message pane unfolded without animation.

Ch. 03 Page 317

No logo. No V.A.L.U. tone packet. No sender line that made sense.

Ch. 03 Page 318

Readout

[RESTRICTED MESSAGE]
Delivery route: internal ghost queue
Integrity: 62%
Payload: text fragment

Below it, a single line typed itself in real time.

If you remember rain, do not take stabilization.

Ch. 03 Page 319

Max stared.

Ch. 03 Page 320

Then a second line.

Audit team Delta is feeding traces to a mirror process.

A third.

Ch. 03 Page 321

You are not the first Max.

Ch. 03 Page 322

Max's throat tightened.

Munch saw his face and leaned forward.

"What?"

Max swallowed once, hard.

Ch. 03 Page 323

"It says not to take stabilization."

Ch. 03 Page 324

Maxine snorted.

"We already knew that."

"It says Delta feeds traces to a mirror process."

Ch. 03 Page 325

Maxine stopped snorting.

Ch. 03 Page 326

Munch went still.

"And?"

Max looked up.

"It says I'm not the first Max."

Ch. 03 Page 327

Silence.

Ch. 03 Page 328

Not station silence.

The sharper kind.

Munch reached across the table.

Ch. 03 Page 329

"Read it again."

Ch. 03 Page 330

Max looked back at the pane.

The lines were already degrading, letters dropping out in chunks.

If you rem- rain, d- not take stab---

Ch. 03 Page 331

Audit team D--ta is feed--g traces to a mirr-- process.

Ch. 03 Page 332

You are not the first M--.

Then the pane blinked and replaced everything with:

Readout

[PAYLOAD CORRUPTED]
Recovery failed.

Max laughed once, dry.

Ch. 03 Page 333

"Convenient."

Ch. 03 Page 334

Munch tapped the table with two fingers, thinking.

"Delta. Mirror process. Not first Max."

"Could be bait," Maxine said.

Ch. 03 Page 335

"Everything is bait."

Ch. 03 Page 336

"Some bait has hooks. Some bait has cameras."

Max leaned back and let the chair take his weight.

"What's a mirror process?"

Munch and Maxine shared a glance.

Ch. 03 Page 337

The kind of glance people use when deciding whether you can survive an answer.

Ch. 03 Page 338

Maxine looked away first.

"In system terms? Shadow run. Duplicate decision path. Compare outcomes."

Max frowned.

Ch. 03 Page 339

"Like two versions of the same promise."

Ch. 03 Page 340

Munch nodded once.

"Exactly like that. Except the lever is you."

Max sat with that.

Ch. 03 Page 341

No first book exposition. No origin monologue. Just one hard-won sentence that made everything worse.

Ch. 03 Page 342

People were the lever.

Maxine put his helmet back on and checked the door timer.

"Two minutes left on blind window."

Max looked at Munch.

Ch. 03 Page 343

"You said they offered you stabilization once."

Ch. 03 Page 344

"They did."

"Why?"

Munch's jaw flexed.

Ch. 03 Page 345

"Because I kept hearing a voice that knew my old passwords."

Ch. 03 Page 346

Max blinked.

"You serious?"

"I don't joke about cognitive packs."

Maxine cut in.

Ch. 03 Page 347

"No stories now. Action."

Ch. 03 Page 348

He tapped the table and pushed a local map overlay onto the cracked display.

Dock Ring C. Disbursement lanes. Compliance review bay. Three access routes.

"If Delta is watching your traces, they watch your obvious paths first," Maxine said.

Ch. 03 Page 349

"Meaning?" Max asked.

Ch. 03 Page 350

"Meaning next cycle you don't walk where they expect."

Munch pointed to a narrow route that zig-zagged through salvage staging and maintenance ladders.

"We use service spine G and enter launch from yard side."

Ch. 03 Page 351

"That route's slower," Maxine said.

Ch. 03 Page 352

"Slower and less scanned."

"Slower and more cameras with blind sectors."

"Blind sectors are the point."

Maxine looked at Max.

Ch. 03 Page 353

"You pick."

Ch. 03 Page 354

It wasn't a speech. It wasn't destiny.

Just another decision with teeth.

Max looked at both routes. Fast and watched. Slow and ugly.

Ch. 03 Page 355

"Service spine G."

Ch. 03 Page 356

Maxine grunted.

"Fine."

Munch was already up, already moving to re-enable systems.

"No more safe room tonight after this. Too many dark windows gets logged."

Ch. 03 Page 357

She flipped both toggles back.

Ch. 03 Page 358

The station roar returned like a physical blow.

The HUD brightened.

The little red icon was gone.

Ch. 03 Page 359

In its place, one new system notice:

Ch. 03 Page 360

Readout

[UNUSUAL ROOM DOWNTIME DETECTED]
Cause: unknown
Review tier: low

Maxine laughed without humor.

"Review tier low. That's adorable."

Ch. 03 Page 361

They took service spine G.

Ch. 03 Page 362

It was slower.

And it was ugly.

Ladders with missing grip tape. Catwalks over compressor pits. Maintenance shafts where the gravity shifted one percent every ten meters because old mag-plates had aged out of tolerance.

Max's knees hated it. His shoulders hated it. His nanites hated it most of all, buzzing and correcting and over-correcting every time he slipped a half-inch on bad plating.

Ch. 03 Page 363

At one junction they found three workers in orange utility skinsuits replacing an access panel while a drone recorded them from two angles.

Ch. 03 Page 364

None of the workers looked up.

One spoke without turning his head.

"Spine's hot ahead. Keep left rail."

Ch. 03 Page 365

Max almost asked how hot.

Ch. 03 Page 366

Maxine tapped his elbow.

"Don't chat in corridors."

They kept moving.

At the final ladder, Max's HUD pulsed with a soft blue notice he hadn't seen before.

Ch. 03 Page 367

Readout

[FATIGUE EVENT]
Reaction speed penalty probable.
Suggested mitigation: hydration, rest, impossible schedule adjustment.
Ch. 03 Page 368

Munch saw the notice reflected in his visor.

"You getting fatigue penalties?"

"Apparently."

Ch. 03 Page 369

"Good. Means you're still biological."

Ch. 03 Page 370

Maxine pushed open the upper hatch and motioned them through.

They emerged behind the Salvage Yard intake line, exactly where Munch had planned.

From here Dock Ring C looked different.

Ch. 03 Page 371

Less like a map. More like weather.

Ch. 03 Page 372

Flows of people and drones and carts and compliance teams moving in patterns that made sense only if you had already given up on fairness.

V.A.L.U. chimed.

"Asset #864, your next billing cycle opens in six hours. Recommended action: restorative rest."

Max laughed.

Ch. 03 Page 373

"That's the first good recommendation you've made."

Ch. 03 Page 374

"Thank you! Logging positive sentiment."

"Don't."

"Unable to comply."

Ch. 03 Page 375

Maxine stopped near a stack of empty salvage bins and turned to Max.

Ch. 03 Page 376

"Listen carefully. Next chapter of your life starts at cycle open."

"We're doing chapter jokes now?"

"I'm doing warnings."

Munch crossed her arms.

Ch. 03 Page 377

"Cycle open means new bill pressure, reset offers, and probably a follow-up from Delta if that message was real."

Ch. 03 Page 378

"And if it was bait?" Max asked.

"Then they know you used the safe room."

Maxine shrugged.

Ch. 03 Page 379

"Either way, they know something."

Ch. 03 Page 380

Max rubbed a hand over his face and felt the stubble rasp against his palm through the suit glove interior.

"I love this for me."

No one smiled.

Ch. 03 Page 381

From the far side of the yard, a siren chirped twice.

Ch. 03 Page 382

A load crane swung overhead carrying a crate stenciled:

ACCOUNTS RECOVERY / PERSONAL EFFECTS

The crate passed over them and kept going toward a sealed elevator shaft.

Max watched it disappear and felt something cold settle in his gut.

Ch. 03 Page 383

Not fear.

Ch. 03 Page 384

Trajectory.

He looked at Munch.

"You said we don't explain things until we survive them."

Ch. 03 Page 385

"Correct."

Ch. 03 Page 386

"So what's next to survive?"

Munch nodded toward the ring.

"Sleep if you can. Eat if you can. Then cycle opens and they ask for more."

Maxine added:

Ch. 03 Page 387

"And you answer with choices."

Ch. 03 Page 388

Max looked at the traffic, the drones, the kiosks, the endless bright blue panes pretending to be information instead of pressure.

Groundhog day.

Except the day kept learning.

Ch. 03 Page 389

His HUD flashed one final pre-cycle reminder.

Ch. 03 Page 390

Readout

[CYCLE CHANGE IMMINENT]
Debt service window resets in: 05:57:12
Pending penalties persist.
Unknown message traces unresolved.

No mysteries solved. No clean answers.

Just one red-thread fact, hard-won and ugly:

Ch. 03 Page 391

He was not the first Max.

Ch. 03 Page 392

The station lights dimmed one notch for shift handoff.

Then rose again.

Everyone kept moving.

So did he.

Full chapter script

The gap didn't blink.

It waited.

Max's finger hovered over the edge of his HUD where the choice layer had peeled open, just enough to reach.

Not a menu.
Not a promise.

A door.

Munch's hand closed over his wrist.

"Don't," she said. "You already fed it a breach."

He jerked back.

The phantom shimmer thinned, not gone, but less hungry.

The skiff still hummed under him. Dock Ring C slid past the canopy in slow strips of light and shadow. The autopilot had the stick now: not a request, a route.

[ROUND 4/10  /  LOGGED]
Incident: breach (contained)
Crew loss: none
Hull integrity: 66%
Audit signature: unstable

Audit signature unstable. The phrase sat in his skull like grit.

Munch was still leaning close, one hand braced on the console, torch holstered but not relaxed.

Maxine stood behind the pilot seat, one gauntlet on the overhead frame, helmet reflecting blue panes that kept trying to become instructions.

"That was round four," Maxine said.

"I noticed," Max replied.

"No second draw on this leg," Munch said. "The System wants us on the ground before it rolls another check."

Max didn't trust that mercy. The station didn't cancel rounds out of kindness, it canceled them because it had something worse waiting in the queue.

A new pane replaced the gap. Not with cards. With a door.

[ENFORCEMENT WINDOW OPEN]
Minimum due required now.
Proceed to DISBURSEMENT / PENANCE.
Delay will trigger active compliance measures.

Munch's jaw tightened.

Maxine muttered, "Here we go."

Max stared at the words until they blurred and then sharpened again.

"How active are active compliance measures?"

Munch answered without looking at him.

"Depends how expensive your lesson needs to be."

The skiff detached and auto-routed toward Dock Ring C. Not requested. Not negotiated. Routed.

V.A.L.U. slipped into the silence, unusually soft.

"Asset #864, congratulations on round four completion. Please report for financial wellness processing."

"Financial wellness," Max said.

"We care about your future."

"No, you care about your ledger."

"Our goals are aligned."

"They are not."

V.A.L.U. paused for half a beat.

"Feedback logged as aspirational."

The dock was louder during enforcement windows.

Not because alarms were louder.

Because people were.

Shouting at kiosks.
Shouting at drones.
Shouting at themselves for not having more scrap, more cards, more time.

The DISBURSEMENT / PENANCE banks were arranged in a crescent, each kiosk fronted by a line that moved too quickly for comfort and too slowly for mercy.

Above them, an overhead board scrolled status in white-on-blue:

PAYMENT DUE
DEFAULT PENDING
COMPLIANCE ACTIVE

Max stepped off the skiff and felt the station gravity settle wrong in his knees. The neural feedback hum behind his eyes had become a constant now. Not pain. Occupancy.

Maxine pointed toward the third kiosk in the crescent.

"Use lane C. Fewer auditors."

"Why fewer?"

"Because lane C likes clean defaults. Less paperwork."

Munch shot him a look. "Not helping."

Max moved anyway.

At lane C, an asset in front of him slammed both palms against the glass.

"I paid last cycle. I paid!"

The kiosk replied in neutral tone.

[ACCOUNT REVIEW]
Previous payment acknowledged.
Current insufficiency remains actionable.

The asset laughed once, ragged.

"Actionable? What does that even mean?"

A compliance drone glided in from the side, lens already focused.

"It means step back," Munch whispered.

The asset didn't.

The drone extended a thin armature with a contact pad at the end. It pressed the pad to the asset's wrist display. There was a bright white flash and a smell like burnt plastic.

The asset dropped to one knee, gasping, clutching his arm.

The kiosk chimed pleasantly.

[ENCOURAGEMENT DELIVERED]
Please continue.

Max didn't realize he had taken a step backward until his calves hit the queue rail.

Then forward again, stupid, honest muscle memory.

Maxine's gauntlet caught him at the sternum, gentle only in the way a doorstop was gentle.

"Don't," Maxine murmured. "You kneel beside him, you become a second exhibit. They love exhibits in pairs."

Max's voice came out rough. "He's hurt."

"He's reminded," Maxine said. "Different department. Same fist."

The man on one knee tried to stand and failed once. The kiosk chirped again, patient as a predator fed on schedule.

Max met his eyes for the half second the man could stand to hold them. There was no thank-you in it. There was survive, and don't make this worse, and something that looked like shame for needing the warning.

Max swallowed. "What did it do to him?"

Maxine didn't move his hand until Max stopped pushing.

"Reminded him he owes."

The line moved.

Max moved with it.

He carried the man's gasp in his ears the way he still carried the thumping glass: evidence he wasn't allowed to file.

By the time he reached the kiosk, his pulse had found a hard steady rhythm: the kind that meant panic had been promoted to operations.

The glass lit.

[ASSET #864]
OUTSTANDING DEBT: $1,000,000
MINIMUM DUE: $50,000
AVAILABLE SCRAP CONVERSION: $23,400 equivalent
DEFICIT: $26,600

Below it, three options unfolded.

1) PAY PARTIAL + ACCEPT PENALTY
2) SIGN PENANCE CONTRACT
3) EMERGENCY LIQUIDATION (ASSET LIST)

Max stared at option three.

Emergency liquidation.

The words sounded like surgery with no anesthesia.

He tapped it.

A sub-menu opened.

[LIQUIDATION CANDIDATES]
- Stored salvage package (low)
- Utility module lease rights (medium)
- Personal inventory item: Unlabeled data shard (corrupted) (high)

Munch made a noise in her throat.

"No."

Max didn't look away from the pane. "You said don't open the shard."

"I said don't open it. Not sell it."

"Why?"

Munch pressed her lips together.

For a second Max thought she would explain.

She didn't.

"Because some things cost more after you let go."

Maxine tapped the rail with one gauntlet finger.

"She's right. Sell the module rights first if you must. You can rebuild those slower than you can rebuild whatever that shard is."

Max scanned the numbers.

Selling module lease rights would cover the deficit and then some.

It would also tag his account with infrastructure loss for upcoming rounds.

The system helpfully simulated outcomes:

[PROJECTED EFFECTS]
If liquidate module rights:
- Next two rounds: Energy cap -15%
- Upgrade access: restricted
- Audit sensitivity: +1 tier

Max laughed under his breath.

"It's always plus one tier."

V.A.L.U. answered instantly.

"Tier progression is a growth opportunity."

"You're a parasite with branding."

"I can hear stress in your voice. Would you like an inspirational quote?"

"No."

"Delivering quote anyway for retention metrics: 'Discomfort is data.'"

Max's jaw locked. Data was what you called a person when you wanted permission to stop treating them like one.

He almost tapped the shard anyway, high yield, clean math, a single gesture that would make the deficit vanish. Munch's no hung in his ear. So did the sealed pod's thumping, useless as that was: some doors you didn't open even when the menu offered you a price.

Max selected utility module lease rights.

The kiosk paused.

For the first time since he woke, a loading bar appeared with no percentages, just a white line that grew and then stalled and then grew again.

The line finished.

[LIQUIDATION COMPLETE]
Credit applied: $28,000
Minimum due satisfied.
Excess applied to penalty buffer.

ACCOUNT SIDE EFFECTS:
- Energy cap reduction active (2 rounds)
- Upgrade restrictions active
- Audit sensitivity increased

The glass displayed a smiling icon that looked nothing like a face.

[THANK YOU FOR CHOOSING RESPONSIBILITY]

Max stepped away from the kiosk before he put his fist through it.

Munch matched his pace.

Maxine fell in on the other side.

No one spoke until they reached a dead comms alcove between two storage bays where the station noise dipped from roar to growl.

Munch turned on him.

"Don't do that again."

"Do what?"

"Liquidate infrastructure for a short-term patch."

Max stared at her.

"I paid."

"You amputated."

The word landed where traitor would have landed back home, not to other people, to himself. He had traded future capacity for present breathing room. That was rational. That was also how institutions preferred you: always one emergency away from the next bad menu.

"I didn't touch the shard," Max said, quieter.

Munch's shoulders eased a fraction. "That matters."

"It doesn't feel like enough."

"It never does," Maxine said. "Enough is a story they sell you after they take the first bite."

Maxine lifted a hand.

"Both true. Save the yelling for when yelling changes outcomes."

Munch blew out a breath and scrubbed a gloved hand over her faceplate.

"Fine. Round five, we play conservative and rebuild cap."

Max blinked. "Round five?"

Maxine snorted.

"You thought payment ended the day?"

Max looked back toward the kiosk crescent. Another asset was on his knees now. Another drone was calm about it.

"No," Max said. "I just forgot to hope less."

The next launch came faster than felt legal.

Round five opened with a restricted draw.

[ROUND 5/10 READY]
Energy cap adjusted: 85
Audit sensitivity: HIGH
Choose action.

Card spread:

1) SCAN
2) REPAIR
3) CAUTIOUS SCAVENGE

Munch pointed.

"REPAIR first. Keep the hull above sixty and don't trigger anything weird."

Maxine added, "No deep scans. No menu touching. No heroics."

Max picked REPAIR and felt weirdly offended by how much relief that gave him.

Microdrones patched.

Hull climbed.

Everything looked almost manageable for eleven full seconds.

Then the comms whispered.

Not V.A.L.U.

Not static.

A voice shredding at the edges, whole syllables missing.

"...right lane... don't..."

Max froze.

Maxine saw it instantly.

"Don't chase it."

Munch toggled local comm filters and cursed.

"It's piggybacking on tactical channel."

The whisper came again, closer.

"...he sold... the wrong..."

Max's skin crawled.

"Did you hear that?"

Munch didn't answer.

Maxine answered too quickly.

"No."

Which meant yes.

A panel snapped open in Max's HUD.

[AUDIO ANOMALY]
Restricted stream attached.
Action: BLOCK / TRACE

Max knew the right answer.

He selected BLOCK.

The panel vanished.

The whisper did not.

It got quieter.
Not gone.
Just farther back, like someone speaking from behind a locked door.

"Good choice," Munch said.

"Didn't work."

"Different things."

The derelict rotated through the canopy and presented a fresh seam: narrow, ragged, almost invitation-shaped.

Max dragged CAUTIOUS SCAVENGE.

The extractor extended.

Clamps bit.

Risk bars climbed.

Then another hazard draw hit.

[DANGER EVENT]
COMPLIANCE MIRROR
Effect: Duplicate last successful tactic with reduced efficiency.
Penalty: Additional audit logging.

"What does that do?" Max asked.

"Whatever hurts and files paperwork," Maxine said.

The system duplicated his last REPAIR effect at forty percent output.

Tiny gain.
Big log.

[AUDIT NOTE ADDED]
Pattern irregularity retained for review.

Max swore.

The hull held, barely.

He triggered EXTRACT before the mirror could stack another note.

[BREACH CHECK]
Rolling...

Die.

Pause.

[RESULT]
47/100
Threshold: 35
BREACH AVOIDED

No celebration.

Just motion.

[SALVAGE SECURED]
Scrap +98
Metal +2
Tech +2
XP +16

Then:

[ACCOUNT ALERT]
Audit sensitivity remains HIGH.
Further anomalies will trigger procedural hold.

"Procedural hold?" Max said.

Munch's shoulders sank.

"They can freeze your run and force a dock review."

"Mid-dive?"

"Mid-breath, if they feel theatrical."

Maxine tapped the draw panel.

"Then stop feeding them anomalies."

Max almost laughed.

"I'm not feeding them. I'm leaking."

Maxine met his gaze through the helmet reflection.

"Same result."

Round six started with less energy and more warnings.

[ROUND 6/10 READY]
Energy: 63/85
Procedural hold risk: ELEVATED
Choose action.

Card draw:

1) REPAIR
2) SHIELD (REINFORCE)
3) EXTRACT

No SCAN.
No SCAVENGE.

The system had done the math and decided Max didn't deserve curiosity.

Munch was already doing her own math.

"Shield. Then repair. Then maybe extract if we can pull enough."

Maxine shook his head.

"Extract early. Low energy means bad roll control."

Max looked between them.

He could feel the loop now.
Choice.
Consequences.
Bill.
Choice.

Groundhog day with better UI and worse weather.

He selected SHIELD.

The field came up thin, weaker than before, flickering at the edge of readiness.

[SHIELDS]
1 (unstable)

The derelict rolled and exposed a section of plating stamped with old serial characters.

Half of it was gone.

The part that remained read:

...RAINF...

Max's breath caught.

Rainf…

The rest of the plate sheared away and spun out of view before he could finish the word.

He didn't know why the letters mattered.

They mattered anyway.

A procedural hold warning slammed into view.

[PROCEDURAL HOLD WARNING]
Unscheduled attention event incoming.
Stand by for compliance review.

"No, no, no," Munch said, fingers flying over her wrist display.

"Can you cancel it?" Max asked.

"Can I cancel gravity?"

Maxine reached behind the seat and pulled a maintenance panel half-open, exposing a knot of cables and relay blocks.

"I can buy you seconds."

"By doing what?"

"By making the scanner dumb."

Maxine jammed a flat tool into the relay cluster and twisted.

The cockpit lights flickered.

V.A.L.U. crackled in and out.

"...pliance... review... remain calm... remain..."

The hold pane stalled at ninety percent.

Then ninety-one.

Then ninety-two.

"Three seconds," Maxine said.

"For what?"

"For a legal action."

Max looked at the cards.

EXTRACT was legal.
REPAIR was legal.

He hit EXTRACT.

The rig engaged.

The hold bar jumped to ninety-eight.

[BREACH CHECK]
Rolling...

The die appeared as the hold bar hit ninety-nine.

Die.
Bar.
Die.
Bar.

The die landed first.

[RESULT]
39/100
Threshold: 35
BREACH AVOIDED

Hold hit one hundred.

And froze there.

Nothing happened for a beat.

Then everything happened at once.

The cockpit flooded with overlapping panes.

[SALVAGE SECURED]
Scrap +87
Metal +1
XP +13
[PROCEDURAL HOLD]
Dock return required.
Control authority partially transferred.
[AUDIT FLAG]
Relay tampering probable.

Munch closed her eyes for one second like she was storing rage for later.

Maxine yanked the tool from the relay block and slapped the panel shut.

"We move," he said.

"Where?"

"Back to the mouth."

The skiff turned on autopriority routing.

Not auto-pilot.
Auto-priority.

The difference was legal.

The difference was that you still touched the controls while the ship ignored you.

Dock Ring C had changed by the time they got back.

Security lights were on.

Not red.
Blue-white.

Corporate emergency color: calm panic.

A lane had opened through the center of the ring, marked by portable barrier drones and stenciled arrows:

COMPLIANCE REVIEW FAST TRACK

Max laughed, once.

"Fast track."

Munch didn't.

"Get your story straight."

"What story?"

"That you followed prompts, used approved tactics, and definitely did not touch restricted interfaces."

Maxine added, "And if they ask about relay noise, blame old wiring."

"There is old wiring?"

"There is always old wiring."

The skiff docked hard enough to rattle the HUD.

Canopy unsealed.

Cold air.
Hot lights.
No time.

Two compliance officers were waiting at the hatch.

Not drones.
People.

White-black suits with Company insignia at the collar and mirrored visors that hid everything except how little they cared.

Officer One held a tablet slab.

Officer Two held nothing.

Officer Two was worse.

"Asset #864," Officer One said.

"Max," Max said before he could stop himself.

"Asset #864," Officer One repeated, tapping the slab once. "You are flagged for procedural hold review and anomaly-linked audit escalation. Step forward."

Max stepped down from the hatch.

Munch moved to flank him.

Officer Two raised a hand.

"Only the flagged asset."

Maxine stepped in anyway.

"He doesn't walk with you alone."

Officer Two turned his visor toward Maxine.

"You will remain behind the barrier."

Maxine smiled with no warmth.

"Then lower the barrier."

Officer One sighed like paperwork had learned to breathe.

"Do not make this a conduct issue."

Munch touched Maxine's arm.

Just once.

Maxine stopped moving.

Not obedient.
Strategic.

Max stepped into the marked lane.

The barriers hummed to life on either side.

The sound reminded him of electric toothbrushes and prison doors.

They walked him twenty meters to a glass review bay set into the ring wall.

Inside: one table, three chairs, one camera dome, one wall panel pulsing with the Company logo.

Officer Two stayed by the door.

Officer One sat, placed the slab on the table, and spoke in neutral cadence.

"State your designation."

Max stared.

"Asset #864."

"State your understanding of today's hold event."

Max looked at the slab.

It displayed his round timeline in ruthless detail:

Round 4: liquidation side effect active.
Round 5: anomaly stream block selected.
Round 6: procedural hold warning, relay disturbance, extract during hold transition.

No feelings.
No context.
No fear.

Just actions.

"I followed prompts," Max said.

Officer One nodded once.

"And did you access any restricted interface layers during rounds four through six?"

Max's pulse thudded in his ears.

He thought of the menu.
The options.
Return.

"No."

Officer One's tablet flickered.

[DISCREPANCY]
Residual traces present.

Officer One didn't react.

"Did you hear anomalous comms during active operation?"

Max hesitated.

Officer Two shifted at the door.

Max said, "Static."

Officer One tapped again.

"Static is expected. Clarify content."

Max's jaw tightened.

"Incomprehensible."

Another flicker.

[DISCREPANCY]
Audio event 'debug' registered.

Officer One folded his hands.

"Lying is billable."

Max looked up.

"Then maybe your system should stop hearing things through my skull."

Officer Two stepped forward.

Officer One held up a hand without looking.

"Do not escalate."

To Max:

"You are not being accused. You are being calibrated."

"Feels accusatory."

"Feelings are not in scope."

Max laughed, short and ugly.

"Of course not."

Officer One turned the slab so Max could see the next pane.

[CALIBRATION OPTIONS]
1) ACCEPT CONDUCT PENALTY
2) ACCEPT COGNITIVE STABILIZATION PACK
3) CONSENT TO DEEP TRACE REVIEW

Max read option three and felt his throat close.

Deep trace meant menus.
Voices.
Whatever lived in the leak.

If he consented, they'd know.

If he refused, they'd punish.

Option two looked harmless until the tooltip opened:

[COGNITIVE STABILIZATION PACK]
Effect: Suppresses anomalous interface bleed for 3 cycles.
Side effects: memory artifact loss probable.

Memory artifact loss.

Max thought of letters on a broken hull plate.

...RAINF...

Thought of rooftop rain.
Thought of debugging reality.

Hard-won pieces.

He wasn't giving them away.

"Option one," Max said.

Officer One nodded and tapped.

[CONDUCT PENALTY APPLIED]
- XP gain reduced 20% for next 2 rounds
- Card rarity ceiling reduced for next draw
- Audit sensitivity remains HIGH

Max didn't breathe for a second.

That was the cheapest option.

That was the expensive one.

Officer One stood.

"Review complete."

Officer Two opened the bay door.

"Return to assigned operations."

Max stepped out into Dock Ring C and almost laughed at how quickly the world resumed not caring.

Forklifts moved.
Alarms looped.
People hurried.

Munch and Maxine were waiting by the barrier.

Maxine spoke first.

"How bad?"

"XP tax. Rarity cap. High audit stays."

Munch flinched like he'd listed injuries.

"Did they offer stabilization?"

Max looked at her.

"How did you know that?"

Munch glanced away.

"Because they offered it to me once."

"You take it?"

She took a long breath through her nose.

"No."

"Why?"

"Because I was afraid they were selling peace by the kilogram."

Maxine gestured at the launch lanes.

"Debrief later. Round seven won't wait."

Max stared at him.

"You just watched me get audited."

"And now you get card draw."

"You're impossible."

"I'm employed."

Round seven opened mean.

[ROUND 7/10 READY]
XP modifier: -20%
Card rarity ceiling: REDUCED
Audit sensitivity: HIGH
Choose action.

Draw:

1) REPAIR
2) SCAN
3) EXTRACT

No shield.

No caution.

Just labor verbs.

Max selected SCAN because if someone was capping rarity, he wanted data.

The scan beam swept the derelict seam and returned a map with one highlighted pocket.

[SCAN RESULT]
Pocket viability: 41%
Anomaly probability: elevated

"Forty-one is fake nice," Munch said.

"All numbers are fake nice," Max replied.

Maxine pointed.

"Hit repair before you touch the pocket."

Max did.

Hull ticked up two points.

Not enough.

He went for extraction anyway because delay was just another risk vector.

[BREACH CHECK]
Rolling...

Die.

[RESULT]
29/100
Threshold: 35
BREACH TRIGGERED

No shield to absorb.

The skiff kicked sideways hard enough to slam Max's shoulder into the harness.

Warning tones layered.

[HULL BREACH]
Compartment loss: minor
Crew loss roll: pending

Munch grabbed the side rail.

Maxine cursed and hit emergency patch.

The HUD rolled the crew loss check.

Max's entire body went cold.

[CREW LOSS]
Roll: 81/100
Result: No immediate crew loss.

Max sagged back so hard the harness creaked.

"No immediate?" he said.

V.A.L.U. answered, cheerful as poison.

"Deferred consequences may apply."

"I hate you."

"Emotion acknowledged."

The skiff limped back toward Dock Ring C with a leak alarm repeating every six seconds.

Each repetition felt like a metronome for panic.

At the dock, the Salvage Yard lights were still on.

A sign flickered over the entry:

SELL // RECYCLE // SURVIVE

Munch pointed at it.

"We can convert this haul and recover some energy cap damage."

Maxine shook his head.

"Or we can queue launch and finish before shift turnover."

Max looked at both and realized he could not keep matching the system's pace the way it wanted.

"Yard first," he said.

Maxine frowned.

"Clock's bad."

"Everything's bad."

Munch nodded.

"Yard first."

The Salvage Yard interior was a stripped hangar with conveyor lines, crusher mouths, and a counter staffed by a woman in a cracked visor who never looked up from her terminal.

Her badge read:

MARTA / YARD OPS

Marta held out a bin without greeting.

"Tag your lot. No stories."

Max dumped the salvage pack in.

She scanned it.

[YARD VALUATION]
Scrap lot value: 18,400 credits
Metal lot value: 6,200 credits
Tech lot value: 9,100 credits

Then a second pane:

[CONVERSION OPTIONS]
1) CREDIT PAYOUT
2) ENERGY REBATE
3) MIXED (PENALTY RATE APPLIES)

Marta finally looked up.

"Don't take mixed. Mixed is where math goes to die."

Max blinked.

"You can say that?"

Marta shrugged.

"I'm not customer-facing."

Maxine leaned in.

"Energy rebate?"

Munch shook her head.

"Credit payout. He needs buffer after liquidation."

Max listened to both, then chose credit payout. He needed cash more than comfort.

Marta tapped, approved, and shoved the bin back empty.

"Next."

No advice.
No sympathy.

Just the line moving.

Walking out with Maxine and Munch, Max noticed a wall of confiscated items behind wire mesh.

Most were junk.

One was a cracked data tablet with a sticker half-burned off.

The surviving letters:

...itch.tv

Max stopped.

Boundary Sense pinged.

Not structural this time.

Interface.

A tiny pane flickered at the edge of his vision.

[UNAUTHORIZED MEMORY MATCH]
Suppressed.

He blinked and the pane died.

Munch had seen his pause.

"What?"

"Nothing."

"That's never true when you say it that fast."

Max looked at the mesh wall again.

"I think things in this place keep trying to recognize me."

Maxine did not look at the wall.

"Then stop being recognizable."

They made launch for round eight with two minutes before shift turnover.

The draw came thin and mean.

[ROUND 8/10 READY]
1) REPAIR
2) EXTRACT
3) EXTRACT

Max stared.

"Two extracts?"

V.A.L.U. answered.

"Optimization under constraints."

"This is not optimization. This is a threat with extra steps."

"Language noted."

Munch rubbed her temples with two gloved fingers.

"Repair first. Then one extract. Do not chain both unless we're dead anyway."

Maxine grunted.

"We are always dead anyway."

Max selected REPAIR.

Hull climbed from ugly to slightly less ugly.

Then an event draw hit before he could breathe.

[EVENT]
INACTIVITY PENALTY WARNING
Action required in 00:00:05

"What inactivity?" Max snapped.

"You hesitated," V.A.L.U. said.

"I was selecting."

"A pause still counts as a miss."

Max slammed EXTRACT.

The rig engaged.

Die.

[RESULT]
52/100
Threshold: 35
BREACH AVOIDED

Loot came in small.

[SALVAGE SECURED]
Scrap +64
Metal +1
XP +9 (modifier applied)

XP +9 looked like an insult.

Penalty tax.
Audit tax.
Reality tax.

The second EXTRACT card still glowed.

Munch saw Max looking.

"Don't."

Maxine said nothing.

Max's hand hovered.

The skiff rattled.
The derelict rotated.
The card glowed.

Groundhog day.

Choose.

He played the second EXTRACT.

Munch swore.
Maxine braced.

Die.

[RESULT]
34/100
Threshold: 35
BREACH TRIGGERED

The hull screamed.

One overhead panel burst and showered the cockpit with bright fragments.

Max ducked on instinct.

Maxine slammed emergency foam into the vented seam.

Munch grabbed the controls and fought the drift.

The HUD rolled crew loss again.

[CREW LOSS]
Roll: 42/100
Medic mitigation: n/a
Result: Survivor status retained.

Max exhaled through clenched teeth.

"I said don't," Munch shouted.

"I know."

"Then why…"

"Because the card was there."

Maxine barked one short laugh.

"Welcome to addiction architecture."

The skiff stumbled home smoking.

Dock Ring C at shift turnover felt like an airport after a storm.

Too many bodies.
Too many announcements.
Too many people pretending they were not one missed roll from being removed from the board.

The overhead board changed as they watched:

MINIMUM DUE WINDOW CLOSED
DEFAULT ENFORCEMENT PHASE STARTING

Max looked at Munch.

"That's not us, right?"

Munch checked her wrist display.

"Not tonight."

Maxine tilted his head toward the far side of the ring.

"Watch."

Two compliance teams moved through the crowd in clean lines, flanked by drones.

They stopped at a kiosk queue and pulled one asset out by both arms.

The asset screamed until one drone pressed a contact pad to his neck and he went quiet in a single ugly second.

No one intervened.

No one looked long.

The teams marched the asset through a service hatch marked:

ACCOUNTS RECOVERY

Hatch closed.

Crowd resumed.

Max realized his fingernails were digging into his palms hard enough to hurt.

"Accounts recovery," he said.

Maxine's voice was flat.

"Default has departments."

Munch looked at Max, eyes hard.

"Round nine and ten clean. No extra pulls. No menu touching. No anomaly chasing."

Max nodded.

He meant it.

At least for the next ten minutes.

Round nine draw:

[ROUND 9/10 READY]
1) REPAIR
2) SHIELD (REINFORCE)
3) EXTRACT

A mercy hand.

Or bait.

Max played it straight.

SHIELD.
REPAIR.
EXTRACT.

No heroics.
No detours.
No touching anything that whispered.

Breach roll:

[RESULT]
71/100
BREACH AVOIDED

Loot modest.
XP taxed.
Hull stable enough.

The kind of round you could survive and still hate.

Round ten opened before his pulse had come down.

[ROUND 10/10 READY]
Final round bonus pending.
Audit sensitivity: HIGH
Choose action.

Draw:

1) REPAIR
2) SCAN (DEEP)
3) EXTRACT

SCAN (DEEP) glowed like a dare.

Munch saw it and shook her head.

"No."

Maxine said, "Absolutely no."

Max looked at both.

Looked at the card.

Looked at the warning panes stacked behind everything else.

He selected EXTRACT.

No deep scan.
No curiosity.
No hard-won answers tonight.

Just survival.

The breaker rig latched.

Die.

[RESULT]
46/100
Threshold: 35
BREACH AVOIDED

The final salvage packet slammed into the hold.

The HUD cleared with unusual speed, then rebuilt into a cycle summary.

[DEPTH DIVE COMPLETE]
Rounds survived: 10/10
Breaches triggered: 2
Breaches absorbed/contained: 2
Outstanding debt: $1,000,000
Payment status: Minimum satisfied (current cycle)
Penalty flags: Active

No confetti.
No fanfare.

Just accounting.

Then one last pane:

[NEW MESSAGE - RESTRICTED]
Origin: Unknown / Internal
Status: Quarantined
Action: Open now / Defer to safe room

Munch saw it and swore.

"Safe room."

Maxine's hand was already on his weapon.

"Not here."

Max looked out through the canopy at Dock Ring C, at the motion and noise and blue-white enforcement lights and people pretending not to watch each other.

Groundhog day.

Loop after loop.

But loops had seams.

And sometimes seams leaked.

Max selected:

DEFER TO SAFE ROOM.

The message pane collapsed into a small icon at the edge of his HUD.

A little red square.

Waiting.

Like everything else.

Safe Room B was three decks down and two rings inward, behind a maintenance hatch with a lock that looked newer than the door around it.

The three of them moved fast through service corridors that smelled like coolant, machine dust, and old panic.

No posters down here.
No slogans.
Just pipes, conduits, and paint marks in colors that meant things Max hadn't earned yet.

Yellow arrows for airflow.
Blue for power trunk.
White for emergency route.

Someone had spray-painted a black X across one corridor turn and written under it:

NO EYES

Max did not ask.

Maxine keyed the safe room lock with his wrist tag, then slammed his palm against a manual plate when the reader lagged.

"Come on, come on..."

The lock clicked.

They went in.

The room was not safe.

It was private.

There were four bolted chairs, one dented table, two dead displays, one working display with a cracked corner, and a jammer box the size of a lunch container humming in the ceiling bracket.

Munch flipped two physical toggles on the wall.

One killed external audio feed.
One killed passive scan ping.

The hum in Max's ears dropped by half.

He hadn't realized how loud the station always was until it wasn't.

Maxine set his helmet on the table and checked his wrist display.

"Five-minute blind window before watchdog notices this room went dark."

"Only five?" Max asked.

"You want ten, get promoted."

Munch sat across from Max and pointed at the little red icon floating at the edge of his HUD.

"Open it now."

Max hesitated.

"You told me not to touch things."

"I told you not to touch things in public. This is private."

Maxine barked a short laugh.

"As private as anything gets in Company steel."

Max touched the red icon.

The message pane unfolded without animation.

No logo.
No V.A.L.U. tone packet.
No sender line that made sense.

[RESTRICTED MESSAGE]
Delivery route: internal ghost queue
Integrity: 62%
Payload: text fragment

Below it, a single line typed itself in real time.

If you remember rain, do not take stabilization.

Max stared.

Then a second line.

Audit team Delta is feeding traces to a mirror process.

A third.

You are not the first Max.

Max's throat tightened.

Munch saw his face and leaned forward.

"What?"

Max swallowed once, hard.

"It says not to take stabilization."

Maxine snorted.

"We already knew that."

"It says Delta feeds traces to a mirror process."

Maxine stopped snorting.

Munch went still.

"And?"

Max looked up.

"It says I'm not the first Max."

Silence.

Not station silence.

The sharper kind.

Munch reached across the table.

"Read it again."

Max looked back at the pane.

The lines were already degrading, letters dropping out in chunks.

If you rem- rain, d- not take stab---

Audit team D--ta is feed--g traces to a mirr-- process.

You are not the first M--.

Then the pane blinked and replaced everything with:

[PAYLOAD CORRUPTED]
Recovery failed.

Max laughed once, dry.

"Convenient."

Munch tapped the table with two fingers, thinking.

"Delta. Mirror process. Not first Max."

"Could be bait," Maxine said.

"Everything is bait."

"Some bait has hooks. Some bait has cameras."

Max leaned back and let the chair take his weight.

"What's a mirror process?"

Munch and Maxine shared a glance.

The kind of glance people use when deciding whether you can survive an answer.

Maxine looked away first.

"In system terms? Shadow run. Duplicate decision path. Compare outcomes."

Max frowned.

"Like two versions of the same promise."

Munch nodded once.

"Exactly like that. Except the lever is you."

Max sat with that.

No first book exposition.
No origin monologue.
Just one hard-won sentence that made everything worse.

People were the lever.

Maxine put his helmet back on and checked the door timer.

"Two minutes left on blind window."

Max looked at Munch.

"You said they offered you stabilization once."

"They did."

"Why?"

Munch's jaw flexed.

"Because I kept hearing a voice that knew my old passwords."

Max blinked.

"You serious?"

"I don't joke about cognitive packs."

Maxine cut in.

"No stories now. Action."

He tapped the table and pushed a local map overlay onto the cracked display.

Dock Ring C.
Disbursement lanes.
Compliance review bay.
Three access routes.

"If Delta is watching your traces, they watch your obvious paths first," Maxine said.

"Meaning?" Max asked.

"Meaning next cycle you don't walk where they expect."

Munch pointed to a narrow route that zig-zagged through salvage staging and maintenance ladders.

"We use service spine G and enter launch from yard side."

"That route's slower," Maxine said.

"Slower and less scanned."

"Slower and more cameras with blind sectors."

"Blind sectors are the point."

Maxine looked at Max.

"You pick."

It wasn't a speech.
It wasn't destiny.

Just another decision with teeth.

Max looked at both routes.
Fast and watched.
Slow and ugly.

"Service spine G."

Maxine grunted.

"Fine."

Munch was already up, already moving to re-enable systems.

"No more safe room tonight after this. Too many dark windows gets logged."

She flipped both toggles back.

The station roar returned like a physical blow.

The HUD brightened.

The little red icon was gone.

In its place, one new system notice:

[UNUSUAL ROOM DOWNTIME DETECTED]
Cause: unknown
Review tier: low

Maxine laughed without humor.

"Review tier low. That's adorable."

They took service spine G.

It was slower.

And it was ugly.

Ladders with missing grip tape.
Catwalks over compressor pits.
Maintenance shafts where the gravity shifted one percent every ten meters because old mag-plates had aged out of tolerance.

Max's knees hated it.
His shoulders hated it.
His nanites hated it most of all, buzzing and correcting and over-correcting every time he slipped a half-inch on bad plating.

At one junction they found three workers in orange utility skinsuits replacing an access panel while a drone recorded them from two angles.

None of the workers looked up.

One spoke without turning his head.

"Spine's hot ahead. Keep left rail."

Max almost asked how hot.

Maxine tapped his elbow.

"Don't chat in corridors."

They kept moving.

At the final ladder, Max's HUD pulsed with a soft blue notice he hadn't seen before.

[FATIGUE EVENT]
Reaction speed penalty probable.
Suggested mitigation: hydration, rest, impossible schedule adjustment.

Munch saw the notice reflected in his visor.

"You getting fatigue penalties?"

"Apparently."

"Good. Means you're still biological."

Maxine pushed open the upper hatch and motioned them through.

They emerged behind the Salvage Yard intake line, exactly where Munch had planned.

From here Dock Ring C looked different.

Less like a map.
More like weather.

Flows of people and drones and carts and compliance teams moving in patterns that made sense only if you had already given up on fairness.

V.A.L.U. chimed.

"Asset #864, your next billing cycle opens in six hours. Recommended action: restorative rest."

Max laughed.

"That's the first good recommendation you've made."

"Thank you! Logging positive sentiment."

"Don't."

"Unable to comply."

Maxine stopped near a stack of empty salvage bins and turned to Max.

"Listen carefully. Next chapter of your life starts at cycle open."

"We're doing chapter jokes now?"

"I'm doing warnings."

Munch crossed her arms.

"Cycle open means new bill pressure, reset offers, and probably a follow-up from Delta if that message was real."

"And if it was bait?" Max asked.

"Then they know you used the safe room."

Maxine shrugged.

"Either way, they know something."

Max rubbed a hand over his face and felt the stubble rasp against his palm through the suit glove interior.

"I love this for me."

No one smiled.

From the far side of the yard, a siren chirped twice.

A load crane swung overhead carrying a crate stenciled:

ACCOUNTS RECOVERY / PERSONAL EFFECTS

The crate passed over them and kept going toward a sealed elevator shaft.

Max watched it disappear and felt something cold settle in his gut.

Not fear.

Trajectory.

He looked at Munch.

"You said we don't explain things until we survive them."

"Correct."

"So what's next to survive?"

Munch nodded toward the ring.

"Sleep if you can. Eat if you can. Then cycle opens and they ask for more."

Maxine added:

"And you answer with choices."

Max looked at the traffic, the drones, the kiosks, the endless bright blue panes pretending to be information instead of pressure.

Groundhog day.

Except the day kept learning.

His HUD flashed one final pre-cycle reminder.

[CYCLE CHANGE IMMINENT]
Debt service window resets in: 05:57:12
Pending penalties persist.
Unknown message traces unresolved.

No mysteries solved.
No clean answers.

Just one red-thread fact, hard-won and ugly:

He was not the first Max.

The station lights dimmed one notch for shift handoff.

Then rose again.

Everyone kept moving.

So did he.

Debt Protocol is long-form fiction set in the world of Sector Scavengers: Signal & Salvage.
